nodes.name: British Monarchs
nodes.description: Key British monarchs from William the Conqueror to present

items.prompt: Which Duke of Normandy defeated Harold Godwinson at the Battle of Hastings on October 14, 1066, becoming the first Norman king of England?
items.explanation:
First Norman king of England (1066–1087), crowned Christmas Day 1066 at Westminster Abbey
Defeated Anglo-Saxon King Harold Godwinson at the Battle of Hastings on October 14, 1066 — the last successful invasion of England
Commissioned the Domesday Book (1086), a comprehensive survey of English landholdings used for taxation — the most detailed census in Europe until the 19th century
Built the Tower of London (the White Tower) as a symbol of Norman power and control
Introduced feudalism to England, redistributing nearly all Anglo-Saxon land to Norman lords
Spoke only Norman French; the English language absorbed thousands of French words as a result of the conquest
Died in Rouen, France, in 1087 after his horse stumbled on burning embers during the sack of Mantes

items.prompt: Which 12th-century English king, famed as a warrior-crusader, spent only about six months of his ten-year reign in England and died from a crossbow wound at a French siege?
items.explanation:
King of England (1189–1199), House of Plantagenet (Angevin)
Led the Third Crusade (1189–1192) alongside Philip II of France and Frederick Barbarossa; won key victories at Acre and Arsuf against Saladin
Spent only about six months of his entire reign in England — spent most of his time campaigning in France and the Holy Land
Captured by Duke Leopold V of Austria on his return from the Crusade; held for ransom by Holy Roman Emperor Henry VI
England paid a staggering 150,000 marks (roughly 35 tons of silver) to ransom him — nearly bankrupting the kingdom
Killed by a crossbow bolt at the siege of Châlus-Chabrol in France on April 6, 1199, at age 41
Forgave the crossbowman who shot him on his deathbed, though the man was reportedly flayed alive afterward

items.prompt: Which English king was forced by rebellious barons to seal the Magna Carta at Runnymede in June 1215, establishing limits on royal authority?
items.explanation:
King of England (1199–1216), House of Plantagenet (Angevin); youngest son of Henry II and Eleanor of Aquitaine
Sealed the Magna Carta at Runnymede on June 15, 1215 — a foundational document for constitutional law and the principle that the king is subject to law
Lost Normandy and most English holdings in France to Philip II, earning the nickname 'Lackland' (also 'Softsword')
Traditional villain in the Robin Hood legends, portrayed as the villainous usurper while his brother was away on Crusade
Excommunicated by Pope Innocent III in 1209 over a dispute about the appointment of the Archbishop of Canterbury
First English king to lose a civil war to his own barons (the First Barons' War, 1215–1217)
Died of dysentery in October 1216 while campaigning against rebel barons; allegedly lost the Crown Jewels in the Wash

items.prompt: Which tall English king, nicknamed 'Longshanks,' conquered Wales in the 1280s and earned the Latin title Malleus Scotorum for his brutal campaigns against Scotland?
items.explanation:
King of England (1272–1307), House of Plantagenet; nicknamed 'Longshanks' for his imposing height (~6'2")
Conquered Wales (1282–1283) and built a ring of massive castles — including Caernarfon, Conwy, and Harlech — to enforce English rule
Earned the epithet 'Hammer of the Scots' (Malleus Scotorum) for his relentless military campaigns against Scotland
Seized the Stone of Scone (Stone of Destiny) from Scotland in 1296 and brought it to Westminster Abbey
Established the Model Parliament (1295), considered a precursor to modern parliamentary government
Expelled all Jews from England in 1290 — they were not formally readmitted until 1656 under Oliver Cromwell
Died in 1307 at Burgh by Sands while marching north for yet another campaign against Scotland

items.prompt: Which 14th-century English king started the Hundred Years' War by claiming the French throne and founded the Order of the Garter, England's highest order of chivalry?
items.explanation:
King of England (1327–1377), House of Plantagenet; reigned 50 years, one of the longest in medieval English history
Claimed the French throne through his mother Isabella (daughter of Philip IV of France), igniting the Hundred Years' War in 1337
Presided over the great English victories at Crécy (1346) and Poitiers (1356, won by his son the Black Prince)
Founded the Order of the Garter in 1348 — the oldest and most senior British order of chivalry, still awarded today
His reign saw the Black Death (1348–1349), which killed roughly one-third to one-half of England's population
Father of Edward the Black Prince, who predeceased him, leading to the succession of his grandson Richard II
Treaty of Brétigny (1360) gave England huge territorial gains in France, including Aquitaine and Calais

items.prompt: Which House of Lancaster king won a legendary victory against a much larger French army at Agincourt in 1415, despite being heavily outnumbered?
items.explanation:
King of England (1413–1422), House of Lancaster
Won the Battle of Agincourt on October 25, 1415, defeating a much larger French force — English longbowmen decimated the French cavalry
Treaty of Troyes (1420) recognized him as regent and heir to the French throne, disinheriting the Dauphin
Married Catherine of Valois, daughter of the French King Charles VI, as part of the Treaty of Troyes settlement
Died of dysentery on August 31, 1422, at Bois de Vincennes near Paris, aged just 35 — two months before Charles VI, meaning he never inherited the French crown
Shakespeare immortalized him in *Henry V* with the St. Crispin's Day speech: 'We few, we happy few, we band of brothers'
As a young prince, reportedly wild and dissolute (Shakespeare's 'Prince Hal' in the *Henry IV* plays)

items.prompt: Which king, the last of his dynasty, was killed at the Battle of Bosworth Field in 1485, and whose remains were discovered beneath a Leicester car park in 2012?
items.explanation:
King of England (1483–1485), last king of the House of York and the last Plantagenet monarch
Killed at the Battle of Bosworth Field on August 22, 1485 — the last English king to die in battle
Shakespeare portrayed him as a scheming hunchback in *Richard III* ('A horse! A horse! My kingdom for a horse!')
Accused of ordering the murder of his nephews, the 'Princes in the Tower' (Edward V and Richard of York) — still debated by historians
Remains discovered under a Leicester car park in 2012 by University of Leicester archaeologists; confirmed by DNA analysis
Reburied with full honors at Leicester Cathedral on March 26, 2015 — over 530 years after his death
Skeletal analysis confirmed severe scoliosis (curvature of the spine), partially vindicating Shakespeare's physical description

items.prompt: Which Welsh-born claimant defeated Richard III at Bosworth Field and founded a new royal dynasty that would rule England for over a century?
items.explanation:
King of England (1485–1509), founder of the House of Tudor — the first Tudor monarch
Won the Battle of Bosworth Field on August 22, 1485, defeating Richard III and ending the Wars of the Roses
Married Elizabeth of York (1486), uniting the rival Lancastrian and Yorkist claims; the Tudor rose (red and white combined) symbolizes this union
Had a tenuous claim to the throne through his mother Margaret Beaufort, a Lancastrian descendant of John of Gaunt
Spent 14 years in exile in Brittany and France before invading England with a small army
Established financial stability through efficient (some said ruthless) taxation; left a full treasury to his son Henry VIII
Died of tuberculosis at Richmond Palace in 1509; buried in the Lady Chapel at Westminster Abbey, which he commissioned

items.prompt: Which Tudor king married six wives, broke with the Roman Catholic Church, and declared himself Supreme Head of the Church of England in 1534?
items.explanation:
King of England (1509–1547), House of Tudor; one of the most famous monarchs in English history
Married six wives: Catherine of Aragon, Anne Boleyn, Jane Seymour, Anne of Cleves, Catherine Howard, Catherine Parr (mnemonic: divorced, beheaded, died, divorced, beheaded, survived)
Broke with Rome over the Pope's refusal to annul his marriage to Catherine of Aragon; Act of Supremacy (1534) made him Supreme Head of the Church of England
Dissolved the monasteries (1536–1541), seizing vast church lands and wealth for the Crown
Father of three monarchs: Edward VI (by Jane Seymour), Mary I (by Catherine of Aragon), and Elizabeth I (by Anne Boleyn)
Commissioned the building of Hampton Court Palace and expanded the Royal Navy, laying foundations for English sea power
Weighed an estimated 400 lbs by the end of his life; died January 28, 1547, at age 55

items.prompt: Which nine-year-old son of a Tudor king became England's youngest monarch and pushed through sweeping Protestant reforms before dying at age 15?
items.explanation:
King of England (1547–1553), House of Tudor; son of Henry VIII and Jane Seymour
Became king at age 9 — governed by regents: first Edward Seymour (Duke of Somerset), then John Dudley (Duke of Northumberland)
Archbishop Thomas Cranmer introduced the Book of Common Prayer (1549, revised 1552), establishing Protestant liturgy in English
Reign transformed the Church of England from essentially Catholic (minus the Pope) to institutionally Protestant
Tried to exclude his Catholic half-sister Mary from the succession by naming Lady Jane Grey as his heir
Lady Jane Grey reigned only nine days before Mary I seized the throne — the shortest reign in English history
Died July 6, 1553, likely of tuberculosis, at age 15; the last male Tudor monarch

items.prompt: Which Tudor queen, daughter of Catherine of Aragon, earned a grim nickname after burning over 280 Protestants at the stake to restore Catholicism in England?
items.explanation:
Queen of England (1553–1558), House of Tudor; first queen regnant of England (ruling in her own right)
Earned the nickname 'Bloody Mary' for burning over 280 religious dissenters at the stake during the Marian persecutions
Restored Roman Catholicism as the state religion, reversing the Protestant reforms of her father Henry VIII and brother Edward VI
Married Philip II of Spain in 1554 — an unpopular match that provoked Wyatt's Rebellion
Lost Calais to France in January 1558 — England's last continental territory, held since 1347
Daughter of Henry VIII and his first wife Catherine of Aragon; declared illegitimate when her parents' marriage was annulled
Died childless on November 17, 1558; reportedly said 'When I am dead, you shall find Calais written on my heart'

items.prompt: Which unmarried Tudor queen presided over the defeat of the Spanish Armada in 1588 and a golden age of English literature that included Shakespeare?
items.explanation:
Queen of England (1558–1603), House of Tudor; last Tudor monarch
Known as the 'Virgin Queen' — never married, using the prospect of marriage as a diplomatic tool throughout her reign
Defeated the Spanish Armada in 1588, securing England's naval dominance and Protestant independence from Catholic Spain
Her reign (the Elizabethan era) saw a flowering of English drama: Shakespeare, Marlowe, Spenser, and the construction of the Globe Theatre
Daughter of Henry VIII and Anne Boleyn; declared illegitimate after her mother's execution, then restored to the succession
Survived multiple Catholic assassination plots, including the Babington Plot (1586), which led to the execution of her cousin Mary, Queen of Scots
44-year reign brought relative stability after decades of religious turmoil; the Elizabethan Religious Settlement (1559) established a moderate Protestantism

items.prompt: Which Scottish king unified the English and Scottish crowns in 1603, authorized a famous Bible translation, and narrowly survived the Gunpowder Plot?
items.explanation:
King of England (1603–1625) and King of Scotland as James VI (from 1567); House of Stuart; first monarch to rule both kingdoms
Son of Mary, Queen of Scots; became King of Scotland at 13 months old after his mother's forced abdication
Survived the Gunpowder Plot (November 5, 1605): Catholic conspirators led by Guy Fawkes attempted to blow up Parliament; foiled the night before
Commissioned the King James Bible (1611), one of the most influential works of English prose in history
Authorized the founding of Jamestown, Virginia (1607) — the first permanent English settlement in the Americas
Wrote *Daemonologie* (1597), a treatise on witchcraft, and believed strongly in the divine right of kings
Guy Fawkes Night (November 5) is still celebrated annually in Britain with bonfires and fireworks

items.prompt: Which Stuart king's conflict with Parliament led to the English Civil War, his trial for high treason, and his public beheading outside the Banqueting House in 1649?
items.explanation:
King of England (1625–1649), House of Stuart; second son of James I
Believed in the divine right of kings and ruled without Parliament for 11 years (the 'Personal Rule,' 1629–1640)
The English Civil War (1642–1651) pitted Royalists ('Cavaliers') against Parliamentarians ('Roundheads') led by Oliver Cromwell
Defeated by the New Model Army; tried for high treason by a specially convened court — the first time a reigning monarch was formally tried and executed
Beheaded on January 30, 1649, outside the Banqueting House in Whitehall; reportedly wore two shirts to avoid shivering in the cold being mistaken for fear
His execution led to the abolition of the monarchy and the establishment of the Commonwealth under Oliver Cromwell (1649–1660)
Considered a martyr by Anglican royalists; January 30 was observed as a day of fasting and humiliation until 1859

items.prompt: Which Stuart king returned from exile in 1660 to restore the monarchy, oversaw the Great Fire of London, and was known as the 'Merry Monarch'?
items.explanation:
King of England (1660–1685), House of Stuart; restored to the throne in 1660 after the collapse of Cromwell's Commonwealth
Known as the 'Merry Monarch' for his hedonistic court and numerous mistresses
His reign saw both the Great Plague (1665, killing ~100,000 Londoners) and the Great Fire of London (1666, destroying 13,200 houses and 87 churches including Old St Paul's)
Personally joined firefighting efforts during the Great Fire alongside his brother James
Fathered at least 12 acknowledged illegitimate children but no legitimate heir, creating a succession crisis
Founded the Royal Observatory at Greenwich (1675) and granted a charter to the Hudson's Bay Company (1670)
Converted to Catholicism on his deathbed; succeeded by his openly Catholic brother James II, sparking the crisis that led to the Glorious Revolution

items.prompt: Which Dutch stadtholder and his English wife were invited to invade England in 1688 and became its only joint monarchs after Parliament passed the Bill of Rights?
items.explanation:
Joint monarchs of England (William: 1689–1702, Mary: 1689–1694), the only co-regents in English history
The Glorious Revolution (1688): Protestant nobles invited William of Orange (Dutch stadtholder) to depose his father-in-law, the Catholic James II
Parliament passed the Bill of Rights (1689), limiting royal power and establishing parliamentary sovereignty — a cornerstone of constitutional monarchy
Mary II was the eldest daughter of James II; William III was both her husband and her first cousin
James II fled to France without a fight; Parliament declared he had 'abdicated' by fleeing
William continued to rule alone after Mary died of smallpox in 1694
Battle of the Boyne (1690): William defeated James II in Ireland — still commemorated in Northern Irish Protestant culture

items.prompt: Which queen, the last of the Stuart dynasty, presided over the 1707 Acts of Union that merged England and Scotland into the Kingdom of Great Britain?
items.explanation:
Queen of England, Scotland, and Ireland (1702–1707), then Queen of Great Britain and Ireland (1707–1714); last Stuart monarch
The Acts of Union (1707) merged the English and Scottish parliaments into a single Parliament of Great Britain — creating the political entity that still exists
Despite 17 pregnancies, none of her children survived to adulthood — her longest-surviving child, William Duke of Gloucester, died at age 11
Her reign saw the War of the Spanish Succession and the Duke of Marlborough's great victories, including Blenheim (1704)
Succeeded by George I of Hanover (her second cousin) under the Act of Settlement (1701), which excluded Catholics from the throne
Her close friendship with Sarah Churchill, Duchess of Marlborough, and its breakdown was dramatized in the 2018 film *The Favourite*
The last monarch to veto an act of Parliament (the Scottish Militia Bill, 1708) — no monarch has used the veto since

items.prompt: Which Hanoverian king reigned for 59 years, lost the American colonies in the Revolutionary War, and suffered recurrent bouts of mental illness that led to a regency?
items.explanation:
King of Great Britain and Ireland (1760–1820), House of Hanover; longest-reigning British king (59 years)
Lost the 13 American colonies in the American Revolutionary War (1775–1783); the Declaration of Independence lists 27 grievances against him
Suffered recurrent episodes of severe mental illness (historically attributed to porphyria, now debated); became permanently incapacitated in 1810
His son George (later George IV) served as Prince Regent from 1811, giving the Regency era its name
First Hanoverian monarch born in England and to speak English as his native language
Presided over the Napoleonic Wars, including Nelson's victory at Trafalgar (1805) and Wellington's at Waterloo (1815)
Died blind, deaf, and mentally incapacitated at Windsor Castle in 1820 at age 81
items.cardFront: 1760–1820, House of Hanover, lost American colonies

items.prompt: Which 19th-century queen reigned for over 63 years, became Empress of India, and gave her name to an era defined by industrial revolution and imperial expansion?
items.explanation:
Queen of the United Kingdom (1837–1901), House of Hanover; reigned 63 years and 216 days — the longest-reigning queen in world history until surpassed by Elizabeth II in 2015
Became Empress of India in 1876 at Prime Minister Disraeli's urging; the British Empire at its peak covered one-fifth of the Earth's land surface
Married Prince Albert of Saxe-Coburg and Gotha in 1840; his death in 1861 left her in mourning for the rest of her life (she wore black for 40 years)
Nine children married into royal families across Europe, earning her the nickname 'Grandmother of Europe'
Carrier of the haemophilia gene, which spread to the Spanish, German, and Russian royal families through her descendants
The Victorian era saw the Great Exhibition (1851), the expansion of railways, and the height of British industrial power
Died January 22, 1901, at Osborne House on the Isle of Wight; her death ended the Hanoverian dynasty (succeeded by Edward VII, House of Saxe-Coburg and Gotha)

items.prompt: Which monarch acceded to the throne in 1952 at age 25, had her coronation broadcast on television, and reigned for over 70 years until her death in 2022?
items.explanation:
Queen of the United Kingdom (1952–2022), House of Windsor; longest-reigning British monarch at 70 years and 214 days
Acceded to the throne on February 6, 1952, upon the death of her father George VI; crowned June 2, 1953, in the first televised coronation
Reigned through the decolonization of Africa and Asia, the Troubles in Northern Ireland, the UK's entry into and exit from the European Union
Served during World War II as a driver and mechanic in the Women's Auxiliary Territorial Service — the only female member of the royal family to serve in the armed forces
Surpassed Queen Victoria's record on September 9, 2015, to become the longest-reigning British monarch
Celebrated her Platinum Jubilee (70 years) in June 2022, just months before her death
Died on September 8, 2022, at Balmoral Castle in Scotland at age 96; succeeded by her son Charles III
